title: Income vs Expense Reality
fullRewardHint: Answer all {{total}} choices correctly to earn the full reward.
reflectionPrompts: What does a monthly expense higher than income do to your financial stress over time?
Which 2–3 expenses could you realistically adjust first if you were in this situation?

stages.prompt: Your income is ₹18,000 and your total monthly expenses are ₹20,000. What does this gap mean for you?

options.label: It is not a problem if it happens only sometimes
options.reflection: Even if it happens "sometimes", a repeated gap where expenses are higher than income usually leads to borrowing or unpaid bills.

options.label: It is a warning sign that spending must change
options.reflection: Correct. A monthly negative gap is an early warning that spending habits or income level must change to avoid debt.

options.label: Future income growth will automatically solve it
options.reflection: Hoping that future income will fix everything is risky. You still need to adjust current spending or find extra income.

options.label: You can safely delay some bills every month
options.reflection: Delaying bills regularly can lead to penalties, service cut-offs, and stress. It is not a safe long‑term solution.

stages.prompt: What is the safest first step when you notice that expenses are above income?

options.label: Immediately take a short-term loan
options.reflection: Borrowing as a first step creates a habit of using credit for normal expenses. This usually leads to a debt cycle.

options.label: List and track all expenses to find leaks
options.reflection: Yes. Writing down and tracking expenses helps you see where small cuts can close the gap without new debt.

options.label: Ignore the gap and hope next month will be better
options.reflection: Ignoring the gap often makes the situation worse. Early awareness and action protect you from debt build‑up.

options.label: Regularly borrow from friends and family
options.reflection: Depending on others every month can damage relationships and still does not fix the real problem in your budget.

stages.prompt: If this negative gap continues for 6–12 months, what is the most likely outcome?

options.label: More financial freedom because loans give flexibility
options.reflection: In reality, frequent borrowing usually reduces freedom. EMIs and informal debts limit your future choices.

options.label: A growing debt cycle and stress
options.reflection: Correct. When expenses are regularly higher than income, unpaid amounts turn into loans, penalties, and financial stress.

options.label: No big change as long as you manage somehow
options.reflection: "Managing somehow" often hides growing problems. Without action, small gaps become serious debt in the long run.

options.label: Automatic savings will cover the gap
options.reflection: If there is already a gap, there is usually no automatic saving happening. Savings must be planned, not assumed.
